title: Chapter 22: The 3 AM Confession
content: Three o'clock in the morning and the ceiling had teeth.

I'd been staring at it for two hours, watching the shadows rearrange themselves into shapes that looked too much like his jaw, his hands, the space between us that had almost closed. The almost-kiss lived in my body now — a low, persistent hum beneath my ribs that wouldn't quiet. Every time I closed my eyes, I was back in that hallway. His breath on my mouth. The question I'd heard in the fraction of silence before I pulled away.
I threw the covers off.

The dormitory was a crypt of breathing. Meredith's steady rasp. Priya's silence. Somewhere down the hall, a pipe groaned through the walls like something dying. I pulled on my cardigan — the grey one, the one that didn't fit right anymore, the one I kept wearing anyway — and moved through the dark by memory. Past the bathroom with its flickering nightlight. Past the stairwell door that always clicked twice. Down the east corridor where the emergency lighting painted everything in a thin, sulphur wash.
My feet knew where they were going before my brain caught up.
The conservatory.
Our place.

I pushed through the door and the smell hit me first — rotting vegetation, sweet and thick and cloying. Decay wrapped in glass. The orchids were gone. Not gone — dead. Brown stalks drooping in their ceramic pots, petals crumbled to papery husks on the soil. The new groundskeeper didn't know the humidity schedule, didn't understand that some of them needed misting at dawn and darkness at noon. He'd watered them like begonias and left them to burn under the glass.
I pressed my hand to my mouth and breathed through my fingers, but the smell was inside me now. Decay and loss. Everything beautiful, killed by carelessness.

I walked between the planters. The lemon tree in the corner had survived — of course it had, citrus is obstinate — but the beds along the south wall were graveyards. Dead ferns. Dead bromeliads. The climbing jasmine had withered to a brown skein on its trellis, and the air that used to smell like a garden in summer now smelled like a garden after a funeral.
I found our planter. The one with the parallel lines.

Heath had carved them the spring of sophomore year, two thin grooves in the wooden frame where we used to sit and pass the hours I wasn't supposed to have. His knife. His hands. Two lines, side by side but not touching. I'd asked him what they meant and he'd said, *That's us. Close. Never crossing.*
I'd laughed. I used to laugh at things he said. I used to laugh a lot.
I sat down on the planter's edge, the wood cold through the thin fabric of my sleep shorts, and I let the silence have me.
The first thing that cracked was my breathing.

It came in short, shallow pulls, like something was sitting on my chest — and then I realized it was me. I was doing this. Three years of architectural composure, of spine like steel and voice like shaved ice, and my lungs had simply... stopped cooperating. I pressed my palms flat against the planter on either side of my thighs and tried to breathe the way Meredith had taught me during exam season — in for four, hold for seven, out for eight — but the numbers dissolved before I could count them.
The almost-kiss had opened something, and I'd been holding it shut for so long that I'd forgotten there was anything behind the door. But there was. There was everything.
Heath.
The pact.
The girl I'd been before the engagement.
The woman I'd become after.
The fact that I could not remember the last time I had felt real.

I bent forward. My forehead found my knees and I folded like paper, like something that had been standing only because no one had pushed it, and the push had finally come. My hands gripped my own elbows, fingers digging in hard enough to bruise, and I made a sound — not a sob, not yet, just a raw vocalization, the noise an animal makes when the trap finally closes.
Then the tears came.

Real ones. Not the almost-tears of the hallway, not the stinging pressure I'd blinked back at the memorial service or swallowed down in the Vance dining room under my mother's appraising eye. These were full-body, chest-heaving, shoulder-shaking tears. The kind that come from somewhere deeper than grief — somewhere geologic. I gasped between them like I was drowning, each breath a fight for air that didn't seem to exist in this glass room full of dead things.

I was crying for the orchids and I was crying for Heath and I was crying for the parallel lines that never crossed and I was crying for the girl who had sat on this exact planter and felt the sun on her face and believed — actually believed — that she could choose differently than what had been chosen for her. I was crying because I had been performing for three years and the performance was a cage and I had built it myself, plank by plank, smile by smile, lie by lie, and somewhere inside the cage the real me had gone quiet and I hadn't even noticed she'd stopped speaking.

The salt ran down my lips. I tasted it — mineral and sharp and alive, the most honest thing I'd put in my mouth in years. My nose ran. My eyes swelled. I was ugly in the way you can only be ugly when no one is watching, folded in half in a room that smelled like rot and memory, the wet of my tears soaking through the thin cotton of my shorts where my knees pressed against my stomach.

I didn't try to stop it. I didn't wipe my face. I just let it come — the break I'd been holding at arm's length since the blizzard, since before the blizzard, since the moment I'd sat across from my father in his study and said *yes* when every cell in my body had screamed *no*.

I don't know how long I cried. Time had gone soft, unreliable. A minute. Ten. Long enough for the planter to go warm beneath me, for my throat to go raw, for the gasping to slow into something that sounded less like drowning and more like the aftermath of a shipwreck — the quiet after the waves have finished pulling things under.
And then I heard it.
The door.

A thin sliver of corridor light widened across the conservatory floor, then narrowed again as the door swung shut. I didn't look up. I couldn't. My forehead was still pressed to my knees, my breath still hitching, and I felt — with the animal certainty of prey — the presence of another person in the room.
Footsteps. Not hesitant. Not rushing. The measured tread of someone who knew exactly where he was going in the dark.
I looked up.

Heath stood in the entrance, silhouetted by the emergency lighting behind him. The sulphur glow caught the edges of his shoulders, the line of his jaw, the slope of his nose. He was wearing a black hoodie and sweats, his hair the kind of uncombed that meant he'd been running his hands through it for hours. Dark circles under his eyes. Barefoot on the tile. Neither of us was supposed to be here.

He saw me — really saw me, with my face swollen and blotchy and wet, my hair stuck to my temples, my body still curled around itself like something that had been struck — and I watched something move across his face. A fracture. The same kind of fracture I'd felt in my own chest. His jaw tightened and his hands curled at his sides and for one terrible, crystalline second I thought he was going to come to me. Put his arms around me. Say the words people say when someone is crying — *it's okay, I'm here, you're alright* — and I would have to survive that. The kindness. The comfort. The old dynamic, weapon and wielder, with him catching me the way he always used to, the way I always let him, the way that made me feel safe and small and utterly incapable of saving myself.
He didn't move.
He stood there and he watched me cry.

And something in that — the restraint of it, the respect of it, the way he refused to make this about his need to fix what I was breaking — undid me all over again. I sobbed. Once. Sharp and wet and involuntary, the sound ripped from somewhere behind my sternum, and I let him see it. I let him see all of it.

The ruined composure. The swollen eyes. The running nose and the trembling jaw and the absolute, uncompromising wreckage of a girl who had held herself together with wire and varnish for three years and was finally, finally falling apart.
It was the most honest thing I'd shown anyone since the blizzard.

Heath walked toward me. Slowly, like approaching something that might bolt. He crossed the conservatory floor in six strides and sat down on the planter beside me. Close enough that I could smell him — sleepless skin and cedar soap and the faint mineral note of the cold — but he didn't touch me. His hands rested on his thighs. His knees pointed forward. He stared at the dead orchids across the room and said nothing.
We sat like that.
Two broken people in a room full of dead flowers.

The silence held us the way the glass held the rot — encased, preserved, not fixing anything but keeping it from spreading. I could hear my own breathing, still uneven, catching on the ragged edges of each exhale. I could hear his, slow and deliberate, the breathing of someone who was choosing calm the way you choose to walk on a broken leg — not because it doesn't hurt but because the alternative is not walking at all.
I spoke first.
My voice was destroyed. Raw and scraped and barely above a whisper, the sound of something that had been screaming for a long time and was only now running out of air.
"I never chose him over you."
The words fell into the dark like stones into water. I felt them sink.
"I chose the plan. There's a difference." I swallowed. My throat clicked. "But I know you can't feel the difference. I can barely feel it anymore."

He didn't respond. The silence stretched and I let it, because I deserved the stretch. I deserved the suspension. Three years I'd carried that sentence inside me like a splinter, working its way deeper with every month of the engagement, every function where I stood on Aldric's arm and smiled with my teeth and felt the distance between *choosing a person* and *choosing a strategy* collapse into the same flat, airless space.

Heath's hands didn't move on his thighs. His gaze stayed fixed on the dead orchids. I watched his profile — the line of his nose, the set of his jaw, the muscle that jumped once in his cheek and then went still.
He said, "I know."

Two words. Simple. No inflection, no drama, no swell of forgiveness or collapse into acceptance. Just acknowledgment. The plain, undecorated recognition of a truth I had never said aloud, and he had never let himself believe, and now we both knew it was real.
*I know.*
Not *it's okay.* Not *I forgive you.* Not *it doesn't matter anymore.*
Just — I know. I hear you. What you're saying is true.

It was more than I deserved and less than I needed and exactly right, somehow, in the way that only Heath Cruz had ever managed to be exactly right — by refusing to give me the easy thing and instead handing me the true one, even when the true thing was a knife and I was the one holding the blade.

I exhaled. It came out shaky and long, a sound that was almost a laugh but not quite — the breath of someone who'd been clenching for years and had finally, after the first devastating unclench, discovered that the air was still there. That she could still breathe.
We sat.

The conservatory smelled like rot and cedar and the salt of my own tears drying on my skin. The emergency lighting from the corridor gave everything a thin amber wash that made the dead flowers look almost golden, almost beautiful, almost like they were sleeping instead of gone.

Time passed. I didn't track it. The gasping had stopped but the tears hadn't — they came slower now, quieter, sliding down my cheeks in a rhythm that matched my breathing, warm tracks cooling against my jaw. My face ached. My throat ached. My chest ached in the deep, muscular way of something that had been holding tension for years and was finally, painfully, releasing it.
The grey came slowly.

At first I thought it was a trick of the eyes — the amber corridor light shifting — but then I saw it: the faint pearlescence at the eastern edge of the glass ceiling, the first rumor of dawn bleeding through. The grey light touched the dead orchids and turned them silver. It touched the parallel lines on the planter frame and made them glow, just barely, like scars in wet wood.
Heath stirred.

He stood up. The planter creaked without his weight. I looked up at him — I was still sitting, still curled, my composure still in ruins around me like the shed skin of a person I'd been pretending to be — and he looked down.

His eyes moved over my face. The swelling. The redness. The wet tracks. The blotchy skin and the ruined composure and the absolute, incontrovertible evidence that Cat Vance was not made of ice. That she could break. That she had.
He said, "If you want to finish what we started, you know where to find me."
My breath caught.

"But I'm not the boy who walked into the blizzard anymore." His voice was low and even, stripped of performance, stripped of the dangerous edge he'd been wielding since he came back. Just Heath. Just the voice I used to hear across this exact planter, saying things that no one else would say to me, true things, hard things. "And you're not the girl who let me go."
He paused. I watched his jaw work, the muscle in his cheek tighten, and I saw the cost of the next sentence in the way his hands curled and uncurled at his sides.
"So if we do this, we do it as who we are now. Not who we were."
He turned and walked out.

The door opened, let in a slice of corridor light, and closed again. His footsteps receded down the east corridor — measured, unhurried, the sound of someone who'd said what he needed to say and wasn't waiting for a reply.
I sat in the dawn light and felt the earth shift under me.

Not the earthquake kind of shift — nothing so dramatic. A tectonic thing. The slow, grinding movement of something fundamental rearranging itself deep below the surface where no one could see. The new grey light filled the conservatory and the dead orchids turned from silver to ash and the parallel lines on the planter caught the dawn and I understood, with the clarity that only comes after you've been taken apart, what he was offering.
A new pact.
Different terms.

Not the old geometry — him as the weapon, me as the hand that held it. Not the parallel lines, close but never touching. Something else. Something I didn't have a shape for yet. Something between two people who knew exactly how much damage they could do to each other and were choosing to step forward anyway.
Not as who we were.
As who we are.

I stayed on the planter as the dawn spread and the citrus tree threw its first long shadow across the floor. I could still taste the salt on my lips. I could still feel the places where the tears had cooled on my skin. I could still hear his voice saying *I know* and *not who we were* and the question beneath both — the question I wasn't ready to answer and couldn't stop thinking about.
What are the new terms?
I didn't know. But for the first time in three years, I wanted to find out.
